channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Reflect Orbital just got permission to launch its 1st space mirror to orbit. Tens of thousands more could follow - Space
item.description:
1. Reflect Orbital just got permission to launch its 1st space mirror to orbit. Tens of thousands more could follow  Space
  2. F.C.C. Approves Test of Space Mirror to Light Night Sky Despite Outcry  The New York Times
  3. Southern California company wants to bring daylight to dark parts of Earth  KTLA
  4. FCC approves first Reflect Orbital satellite  SpaceNews
  5. FCC Approves Extremely Controversial Space Mirror Satellite That Could "Flash Blind" Drivers And Ruin Astronomy  IFLScience

item.description: Pythons’ extreme biology may hold clues for treating human disease  NPR

item.title: A T. rex found in South Dakota could smash auction records — and possibly disappear forever - CNN
item.description:
1. A T. rex found in South Dakota could smash auction records — and possibly disappear forever  CNN
  2. T. rex could become most expensive fossil ever - but it's a ​problem for scientists  BBC
  3. Gus, the world’s most expensive dinosaur, divides scientists  The Telegraph
  4. Want to own a real T. rex? It could cost you $30 million  NPR
  5. A record-breaking T. rex, an Antarctic fossil, and the climate clues in dinosaur bones  Yahoo

item.description:
1. Natural sugar discovered in cloud of dust and gas near centre of Milky Way  The Guardian

  3. Something surprisingly sweet lurks near the Milky Way’s heart  Scientific American
  4. Sugar discovered floating in deep space for the first time  Popular Science
  5. Sugar molecules found in interstellar space for the first time  New Scientist

item.title: Scientists examining samples returned from asteroid Bennu found ribose and glucose inside — and with phosphate and every RNA nucleobase already detected in the same asteroid material, every chemical component needed to build RNA was present in th - Space Daily
item.description: Scientists examining samples returned from asteroid Bennu found ribose and glucose inside — and with phosphate and every RNA nucleobase already detected in the same asteroid material, every chemical component needed to build RNA was present in th  Space Daily

item.title: Perseid Meteor Shower Returns This Week Under An ‘Earthshine’ Moon - Forbes
item.description:
1. Perseid Meteor Shower Returns This Week Under An ‘Earthshine’ Moon  Forbes

  3. Plan an August backyard campout or road trip around the Perseid meteor shower  Yahoo Creators
  4. Perseid Meteor Shower 2026: Best Places To See The Celestial Display  Travel and Leisure Asia
  5. One of the best meteor showers of the year is about to begin—and it can be seen across the US with up to 100 meteors an hour  MSN

item.description:
1. Physicists recreate black hole energy extraction in the lab  ScienceDaily

  3. Black hole theory: Synthetic ultrafast rotation amplifies electromagnetic waves  Open Access Government
  4. Physicists Replicate Black Hole Energy Extraction Using Synthetic Rotation  Tech Times
  5. CUNY physicists recreate black hole energy extraction in a historic lab experiment  The Brighter Side of News

item.title: Gravitational waves reveal hidden populations within black hole mergers - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Gravitational waves reveal hidden populations within black hole mergers  Phys.org
  2. Big evidence for "second generation" black hole mergers  Big Think
  3. Evidence Mounts for Hierarchical Black Hole Mergers  American Physical Society
  4. Scientists Say Some Black Holes Are Born From Other Black Holes  Gizmodo
  5. Many black holes may not be born the way we thought  Tech Explorist

item.title: Oldest quasars ever discovered add to 'perplexing' space mystery - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Oldest quasars ever discovered add to 'perplexing' space mystery  Phys.org
  2. ESA’s Euclid Space Telescope Finds Universe’s Most Ancient Quasars  NASA Science (.gov)
  3. Ancient quasars add to a 'major unsolved problem' in astrophysics  Reuters
  4. Scientists have discovered the oldest quasar ever seen, and it shines with the light of a trillion suns  Space
  5. Euclid Telescope Finds Quasars Within 700 Million Years of the Big Bang  Sky & Telescope

item.title: When Italian and German researchers modeled a square-meter array of 1,482 neodymium magnets, the simulation showed roughly a fifth of incoming low-energy solar protons being deflected — suggesting a potential way for deep-space crews to reduce som - Space Daily
item.description:
1. When Italian and German researchers modeled a square-meter array of 1,482 neodymium magnets, the simulation showed roughly a fifth of incoming low-energy solar protons being deflected — suggesting a potential way for deep-space crews to reduce som  Space Daily
  2. Could Permanent Magnets Protect Astronauts from Solar Storms?  Universe Today
  3. Can permanent magnets protect astronauts from solar storms?  Universe Space Tech

item.title: Europa, one of Jupiter’s moons, is sealed beneath a shell of ice, but the ocean hidden below may contain twice as much liquid water as every ocean on Earth combined — and Jupiter’s gravity may have kept it liquid for billions of years - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Europa, one of Jupiter’s moons, is sealed beneath a shell of ice, but the ocean hidden below may contain twice as much liquid water as every ocean on Earth combined — and Jupiter’s gravity may have kept it liquid for billions of years  Space Daily
  2. San Antonio Astronomical Association monthly public meeting: Europa Clipper Update  Texas Public Radio | TPR
  3. Could Humans Survive on Europa?  WorldAtlas
  4. A 2026 study found that Jupiter's ocean moon Europa may have a geologically quiet seafloor with little active faulting — meaning the vast hidden ocean long treated as our best bet for alien life may lack the very energy that life would need to survive  Space Daily
  5. In 1610, Galileo spotted a small, smooth moon circling Jupiter and wrote it down; four hundred years later, scientists confirmed it likely holds the largest ocean in the solar system, buried under ice and never once touched by sunlight  Space Daily

item.title: The 25 Best James Webb Space Telescope Photos - PetaPixel
item.description:
1. The 25 Best James Webb Space Telescope Photos  PetaPixel
  2. NASA Webb Uncovers Unusual Galaxy Shaped by Cosmic Collision  NASA Science (.gov)
  3. James Webb telescope captures never-before-seen glimpse of 'Centaur' galaxy's battle wounds — Space photo of the week  Live Science
  4. James Webb Space Telescope celebrates its 4th birthday with stunning image of a galaxy crash site  Space
  5. Space News: Webb uncovers unusual galaxy shaped by cosmic collision  Lake County News,California

item.title: A total solar eclipse is coming in August. Will you be able to see it? - USA Today
item.description:
1. A total solar eclipse is coming in August. Will you be able to see it?  USA Today
  2. 1 month until the total solar eclipse 2026 — Here's what you need to know  Space
  3. This summer's sunset solar eclipse will be like nothing you've ever seen. It's a rare, beautiful sight you won't want to miss  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  4. Total solar eclipse is coming. Michigan's view won't be impressive  Detroit Free Press
  5. Europe's first total solar eclipse in almost 30 years: What you need to know  Sky News

item.description:
1. Scientists Propose Dimming Sun to Combat El Niño  Yahoo
  2. Another Super El Niño is brewing. Scientists are looking at a controversial solution to squash them  CNN
  3. Seeding clouds with seawater could prevent a super El Niño  New Scientist
  4. Race to save Aus from ‘super’ El Nino  News.com.au
  5. Dimming the Sun Would Help Lower the Risks of El Niño. No, Really  WIRED

item.description: Fireball seen hurtling over North Carolina was not a meteor, experts say  Charlotte Observer

item.title: James Webb has caught at least five galaxies colliding about 800 million years after the Big Bang, flinging heavy elements out into space far earlier than any model predicted — evidence the early universe was already messy and mature when it should still ha - Space Daily
item.description:
1. James Webb has caught at least five galaxies colliding about 800 million years after the Big Bang, flinging heavy elements out into space far earlier than any model predicted — evidence the early universe was already messy and mature when it should still ha  Space Daily
  2. Photo: A New Map of the Universe  Yahoo
  3. When did the first galaxies form? Earlier than we thought possible  New Scientist
  4. James Webb telescope's largest-ever map of the universe unmasks hidden corners  Live Science
  5. CONICET researchers reveal details about the formation of massive galaxies in the early universe  Noticias Ambientales

item.title: Chinese Spacecraft Approaches Mysterious Object Near Earth - Futurism
item.description:
1. Chinese Spacecraft Approaches Mysterious Object Near Earth  Futurism
  2. In Deep Space, China Meets Earth’s ‘Amazing’ Quasi-Moon  The New York Times
  3. China releases 1st photo of Earth's elusive 'quasi-moon' Kamo'oalewa  Space
  4. China’s Tianwen-2 Space Probe Has Rendezvoused With Earth’s Quasi-Moon  WIRED
  5. A new picture of Earth’s ‘mini moon’  Coeur d'Alene Press

item.title: Earth’s inner core is not rock but a sphere of solid iron-rich metal, smaller than the Moon yet probably more massive. As the planet gradually loses heat, liquid metal freezes onto its surface, expanding it by roughly a millimetre each year. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Earth’s inner core is not rock but a sphere of solid iron-rich metal, smaller than the Moon yet probably more massive. As the planet gradually loses heat, liquid metal freezes onto its surface, expanding it by roughly a millimetre each year.  Space Daily
  2. Academia Sinica - Research Findings | Earth’s mantle locally heats up our planet’s core  sinica.edu.tw
  3. Earth’s inner core is an iron-rich ball as hot as the surface of the Sun, kept solid only because the crushing pressure of the entire planet raises the temperature at which iron can melt  Space Daily

item.title: The rivers on Titan flow, pool into lakes and are fed by rainfall on a slow cycle much like our own, but the liquid is methane and the ground beneath it is water frozen hard as stone - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The rivers on Titan flow, pool into lakes and are fed by rainfall on a slow cycle much like our own, but the liquid is methane and the ground beneath it is water frozen hard as stone  Space Daily
  2. Titan has rivers, rainfall, lakes and a slow hydrological cycle exactly like Earth's, except that every drop of it is liquid methane and the rock the rivers run over is water ice  Space Daily

item.title: Self‑building molecular rings bring next‑generation drug delivery and smart materials closer - Phys.org
item.description: Self‑building molecular rings bring next‑generation drug delivery and smart materials closer  Phys.org

item.title: A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about four billion tonnes on Earth — roughly the mass of a mountain — because the star has crushed the equivalent of the Sun into a sphere the width of a city - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A single teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about four billion tonnes on Earth — roughly the mass of a mountain — because the star has crushed the equivalent of the Sun into a sphere the width of a city  Space Daily
  2. A neutron star can pack more mass than our entire Sun into a sphere only about 20 kilometres across — roughly the size of a city — and a teaspoon of its material would weigh about a billion tonnes on Earth, more than every human being alive combined.  Space Daily

item.title: Nearly a millennium ago sky-watchers recorded a new star so bright it was visible in daylight for weeks, and Hubble has now traced how far its remnant, the Crab Nebula, has expanded since — a thousand-year-old explosion still visibly unfolding - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Nearly a millennium ago sky-watchers recorded a new star so bright it was visible in daylight for weeks, and Hubble has now traced how far its remnant, the Crab Nebula, has expanded since — a thousand-year-old explosion still visibly unfolding  Space Daily
  2. A supernova lit up Earth’s sky in 1054 and was visible in daylight. Hubble now shows its glowing remains are still expanding nearly 1,000 years later  The Times of India

item.title: A rare meteorite examined in 2026 revealed evidence of a Moon-sized world that orbited the young Sun more than 4.5 billion years ago, then vanished — possibly shattered in a catastrophic collision while Earth was still being assembled - Space Daily
item.description: A rare meteorite examined in 2026 revealed evidence of a Moon-sized world that orbited the young Sun more than 4.5 billion years ago, then vanished — possibly shattered in a catastrophic collision while Earth was still being assembled  Space Daily

item.title: Voyager 1 Has Traveled Through Space at 38,000 Mph Since 1977 And Still Hasn't Reached 1 Light-Day - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Voyager 1 Has Traveled Through Space at 38,000 Mph Since 1977 And Still Hasn't Reached 1 Light-Day  ScienceAlert
  2. NASA is keeping Voyager 1 alive by switching off instruments and heaters one by one—but it cannot let the spacecraft become so cold that its fuel lines freeze. If those lines fail, Voyager could lose the thrusters that keep its antenna aimed at Earth, permanently  Space Daily
  3. Voyager 1 was launched in 1977 with a suite of 10 science instruments — as of 2026, only two remain switched on, and the spacecraft's nuclear power source loses roughly 4 watts every year, a slow drain that is now deciding what humanity still gets to learn fr  ScienceBlog.com
  4. Voyager Spacecraft: The Ultimate Power Management Challenge?  EE Times
  5. One Light-Day Away: Voyager's Epic Voyage  Rediff

item.description: 25 Year Old Building India's Spaceplane  Rediff

item.description: Working with a rod-shaped microbe, she helped spark an agricultural revolution  The Washington Post

item.title: Rescue robot of tomorrow may be a cockroach in scuba suit - Fox News
item.description:
1. Rescue robot of tomorrow may be a cockroach in scuba suit  Fox News
  2. Scientists build tiny 'diving suit' for cockroaches, turning them into search-and-rescue cyborgs  Live Science
  3. Japan lab steers 'insect cyborgs' toward disaster search missions  毎日新聞
  4. World's first 'diving suits' for cockroaches  The Naked Scientists
  5. Cyborg Cockroach Gets 3D-Printed Diving Suit: Flood Rescue Breakthrough Using Biomimetic Insects  Pandaily

item.title: AI Just Uncovered a Hidden Secret Inside Water - SciTechDaily
item.description:
1. AI Just Uncovered a Hidden Secret Inside Water  SciTechDaily
  2. AI Identifies the Most Effective Ways to Measure Water’s Hidden Structure  The Debrief
  3. Fig. 1 (IMAGE)  EurekAlert!
  4. 💧 Water would be made of two liquids in one  Techno-Science.net
  5. Machine learning evaluation of structural descriptors for supercooled water  Nature

item.title: Leafcutter ants don't eat the leaves they carry home in long green columns — they feed the clippings to an underground fungus, and it is the fungus they farm and eat, a practice that predates human agriculture by around 50 million years - Space Daily
item.description: Leafcutter ants don't eat the leaves they carry home in long green columns — they feed the clippings to an underground fungus, and it is the fungus they farm and eat, a practice that predates human agriculture by around 50 million years  Space Daily

item.title: NASA's Psyche spacecraft, on its way to a metal-rich asteroid, used Mars as a gravitational slingshot in May 2026, skimming just 2,800 miles above the planet to gain the speed it needed — Mars as a stepping stone rather than a destination - Space Daily
item.description:
1. NASA's Psyche spacecraft, on its way to a metal-rich asteroid, used Mars as a gravitational slingshot in May 2026, skimming just 2,800 miles above the planet to gain the speed it needed — Mars as a stepping stone rather than a destination  Space Daily
  2. Asteroid metals worth billions: The reality behind the next space rush  Futura, le média qui explore le monde
  3. NASA’s Psyche Spacecraft Passed Within 2,864 Miles of Mars, Gained 1,000 MPH Without Using Onboard Propellant, and Is Now Headed for a Metal World That May Be an Early Planet’s Exposed Core  19FortyFive
  4. The truth behind the $10 quintillion asteroid 16 Psyche | Tap to know more | Inshorts  Inshorts
  5. NASA’s Psyche Tracker: On A Mission To Probe $100,000 Quadrillion Worth Metal-Rich Asteroid  Mashable India

item.title: An asteroid struck somewhere north of Australia 11 million years ago, scattering molten glass across a 900-kilometre ellipse in South Australia — yet scientists still cannot find the crater it left behind - Space Daily
item.description: An asteroid struck somewhere north of Australia 11 million years ago, scattering molten glass across a 900-kilometre ellipse in South Australia — yet scientists still cannot find the crater it left behind  Space Daily

item.title: If you compressed the entire history of Earth into a single calendar year, humans appear at 11:36 pm on the 31st of December, agriculture at 11:59, and everything written down in history occupies the final 15 seconds before midnight - Space Daily
item.description: If you compressed the entire history of Earth into a single calendar year, humans appear at 11:36 pm on the 31st of December, agriculture at 11:59, and everything written down in history occupies the final 15 seconds before midnight  Space Daily

item.description:
1. Vantablack: World's Blackest Paint Could Solve A Major Problem For Astronomy  ScienceAlert

  3. Vantablack, the world's blackest paint, is shown to have the potential to prevent light pollution from satellites.  GIGAZINE
  4. Ultra-Black Coating Reduces Satellite Glare  Optics & Photonics News
